Deelgemeente in East Flanders, Belgium

**Vosselare** is a village and submunicipality of the municipality of [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze"), Belgium in the province of [East-Flanders](/wiki/East-Flanders "East-Flanders"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vosselare&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The parish is first mentioned in 694 as Fursitio in the yearbook of the St-Peter Abbey of Ghent, and again in 802. Later on it is referred to as 'Voslariensis ecclesiae' (1087), Vurslar (1122), Vorselar (1125), Furselare (1130), Vurselaer (1140), Vorselaer (1147), Vursselare (1230, 1320, 1579), Vorslaer (1330), Vurselare (1364, 1500) and Vosselare for the first time in 1650.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2) The name ‘Vosselaere’ is also used.[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3)

Although other explanations are given, the name Vosselare probably originates from 'Vurst' and 'Laar', meaning an open, empty space in a forest.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2)

The village of Vosselare was attacked by [Charles de Valois](/wiki/Charles_of_Valois "Charles of Valois")'s army in 1300. The village was also affected by the battle of Nevele on 13 May 1381.[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3) On 25 May 1452 there was [another battle](/w/index.php?title=Battle_of_Nevele_(1452)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Battle of Nevele (1452) (page does not exist)") near the village, between the city of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), under the command of Jean Van Melle, and the [Count of Estampes](/wiki/John_II,_Count_of_Nevers "John II, Count of Nevers").[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3) [Geuzen](/wiki/Geuzen "Geuzen") attacked the church and a farmhouse on 4 October 1575.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2)[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3) The village was attacked by [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV")'s French troops in May 1673, October 1674, January 1675 and during the siege of Ghent in 1678.[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3) Six thousand French knights occupied the village and destroyed the harvest on 18 August 1684.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2) The church was pillaged twice in 1688, and again in 1691 and 1696.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2)[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3) Mortality also increased between 1676 and 1695 by an (unnamed) epidemic disease.[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3)

### Famous families

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vosselare&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Famous families")]

The [heerlijkheid](/wiki/Heerlijkheid "Heerlijkheid") Vosselare is known from its lord 'Willelmus de Vurslar' or 'de Forselar' (1122, 1125),[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2) while his brother Balduinus de Furslare is mentioned in 1130.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2) The family is also mentioned later as Heinric van Vurselare (1364) and Arnold van Vurselaer (1413)[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2) but the [heerlijkheid](/wiki/Heerlijkheid "Heerlijkheid") was already passed over to Thomas van den Turre, who was mentioned as a participant in the [Battle of the Golden Spurs](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Golden_Spurs "Battle of the Golden Spurs")[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2)[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3) in 1302.

## Monuments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vosselare&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Monuments")]

### Heerlijkheid castle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vosselare&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Heerlijkheid castle")]

Within Vosselare, the [heerlijkheid](/wiki/Heerlijkheid "Heerlijkheid") (or the 'Hof Ter Meeren' or 'Hof ter Mere' castle) is of importance. It belonged to the lords ‘de Meere’ or ‘van der Meere(n)’.[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3) The heerlijkheid covered parts of many neighbouring communities.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2) The castle was destroyed during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), and was rebuilt in 1923–1924.[[4]](#cite_note-nevele-4)

There is also the heerlijkheid castle of the 'Goed ter Meersch', also located in Vosselare. A large farm with the same name still remains. It is supposed to have been a possession of the [Knights Templar](/wiki/Knights_Templar "Knights Templar"), passing over to the Van der Meersch family in 1314.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2) The castle of the Van der Meersch family, located on the site of the current farm, Goed ter Meersch, was destroyed during the Battle of Nevele, 13 May 1381.

### Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vosselare&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Church")]

The church is first mentioned in 1087.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2) Van Hoorebeke argues that it was possibly one of the first churches in Flanders with a belltower.[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3) A new church building is supposed to have been built by the [Knights Templar](/wiki/Knights_Templar "Knights Templar") at the end of their existence, resulting in a close connection between the church and the Van der Meersch noble family, who are supposed to descend from a Knights Templar.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2) The church was severely destroyed in 1940 and was rebuilt in 1953 by the architect Vaerwyck-Suys.[[4]](#cite_note-nevele-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Meerelinde lime tree

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vosselare&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Meerelinde lime tree")]

Previously, there was a large lime tree named ‘Meerelinde’ at the crossroad leading to the Hof ter Mere castle, which was, in the 19th century, referred to as one of the most impressive trees of Europe.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2)[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3) Reports on its circumference at the base vary: 9.20 meters (1856[[6]](#cite_note-AG-6)), 9.75 meters (1845[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3)), or more than 12 meters (1870[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2)). More than 30 people fitted in a cavity in the trunk in 1838.[[2]](#cite_note-Dpotter-2)[[3]](#cite_note-VH-3) The tree had an age of ca. 800 years in the 19th century.[[6]](#cite_note-AG-6)
